text: Many of us find suturing with the robot difficult and challenging, partly because we just can't figure out how to hold the needle. And we're left with the question, is it a forehand stitch? Is it maybe a backhand stitch? Is it some other kind of creative way to hold the needle that we haven't figured out yet? And it's just hard to get the right angles. So here I want to share a way of getting the right angles that's very simple. In your non-dominant hand, just hold the needle where you want it to go. So if we want it to go through the muscle in that direction, we're going to hold it there and then simply grab it with your dominant hand about three quarters of the way back. And then you're simply going to wind up the needle and then unwind it through the tissue. So grab it in the position it's going to go, wind it backwards, and then wind it forwards through the tissue. And it makes it a whole lot easier than trying to figure out what's a forehand and what's a backhand and which way it's going to go. So pretty simple. So let's look at that again. We're going to just position it. Now it's a slightly different position than on the other side of the muscle. We're going to grab it about three quarters of the way back, wind up the needle, and then unwind it through the tissue, just following the curve of the needle, and you'll get it right every time. And we're going to go to all different angles using this, and it helps just the same. So again, we're back to the other side of the muscle, slightly different position, put it where you want it, grab it three quarters of the way back, wind it up, and then unwind it through the tissue. And there you go, and you can do that in just about any situation.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to grab the hernia sac, positioning the needle where it is intended to penetrate. The needle is wound up and unwound through the hernia sac along its curve. The suture is gradually tightened by pulling the top of the suture where the needle exits the muscle, ensuring tension is maintained along the suture line. The barb suture is locked by reversing it over the entire length to secure the suture line against the abdominal wall pressure.

The surgeon switches to sewing the peritoneum using a consistent technique to handle various angles. The needle is positioned vertically across the mesh, grabbing both the mesh and muscle to secure it, then wound up and unwound through the tissue. The needle is then held horizontally, allowing multiple passes without regrabbing, demonstrating efficient suturing in different directions.
